subject: History
chapter: Mahatma Gandhi and the Nationalist Movement

concepts.title: Mahatma Gandhi's Quit India Movement
concepts.description: Mahatma Gandhi launched the Quit India Movement on 8th August 1942, demanding complete independence from British rule. This movement played a significant role in India's struggle for freedom.

concepts.title: Salt March (Dandi March)
concepts.description: The Salt March, also known as the Dandi March, was a 24-day march led by Mahatma Gandhi in 1930 to protest the British salt tax. The march started on March 12, 1930, from Ahmedabad and ended on April 6, 1930, at Dandi, Gujarat.

concepts.title: Non-Cooperation Movement
concepts.description: The Non-Cooperation Movement was launched by Mahatma Gandhi in 1920 to achieve complete independence from British rule. The movement involved boycott of British goods, courts, and law enforcement agencies. It was a significant step towards India's struggle for freedom.

concepts.title: Charkha Symbolism
concepts.description: The charkha (spinning wheel) was a symbol of the Indian Nationalist Movement under Mahatma Gandhi. A mnemonic to remember its significance is: 'C' for 'Charkha', 'H' for 'Handloom', 'A' for 'Agriculture', 'R' for 'Revolution', and 'K' for 'Khadi' (Hand-spun cloth).

concepts.title: Misconception about the Champaran Satyagraha
concepts.description: Many students often confuse the Champaran Satyagraha with the Ahmedabad Mill Strike. However, the Champaran Satyagraha was a movement led by Mahatma Gandhi in 1917 to address the grievances of farmers in Champaran, Bihar. It was his first major satyagraha, and it marked the beginning of his involvement in the Indian Nationalist Movement.

concepts.title: Mahatma Gandhi's Leadership in the Nationalist Movement
concepts.description: To remember Mahatma Gandhi's leadership in the Nationalist Movement, recall the key events and movements he led, such as the Non-Cooperation Movement, the Salt March, the Quit India Movement, and the Champaran Satyagraha. These events showcase his commitment to non-violent resistance and his pivotal role in India's struggle for freedom.

concepts.title: Indian National Congress's Role
concepts.description: The Indian National Congress played a crucial role in India's struggle for freedom. It was initially formed in 1885 as a platform for Indians to interact with the British. However, under the leadership of Mahatma Gandhi, it transformed into a powerful force for Indian independence, participating in various movements and protests against British rule.

concepts.title: Jallianwala Bagh Massacre
concepts.description: The Jallianwala Bagh Massacre, also known as the Amritsar Massacre, was a brutal incident that took place on April 13, 1919, during a peaceful protest in Jallianwala Bagh, Amritsar. The British army, led by General Dyer, opened fire on the protesters, killing hundreds of innocent people. This event marked a turning point in India's struggle for freedom, with many Indians becoming more determined to resist British rule.
